section_title: INTRODUCTION TO THE ICT LONDON CLOSE KILLZONE
content:
This teaching specifically deals with the ICT London Close Killzone. It builds upon ICT concepts,
emphasizing the importance of time and price.

section_title: CHARACTERISTICS OF THE LONDON CLOSE KILLZONE
content:
The London Close is a particular time of day that usually ends the opposite end of the daily range.
- Major pairs, usually coupled with a dollar, are ideal for this time of day.
- The London Close frequently sets up an optimal trade entry pattern that can offer 10 to 20 pips for a scalp.
- The key times to monitor are 10 a.m. to noon New York time. This is defined as the ICT London Close
Killzone.

- DAILY HIGH FORMATION
On a Dollar CAD chart:
- In the beginning of the day, during the Asian market consolidation, the low of the day is created.
- After midnight New York time, the market rallies one-sided all the way up into the time window of 10 a.m.
and noon New York time.
- The daily high is formed exactly within this London Close Killzone window of time.
- Price subsequently trades lower into the close.

- ENCAPSULATING THE DAILY RANGE
On a Euro Dollar chart:
- The Asian range is noted, with a high created during the Asian market.
- After New York midnight time, a high forms again (London high, New York session high).
- The low of the day forms exactly at the parameters between 10 a.m. and noon New York time, which is
the ICT London Close Killzone.
- The market then trades higher off that window of time and goes into the close, demonstrating how the
London Close encapsulates the daily range.

section_title: LONDON CLOSE KILLZONE PRICE ACTION AND OTE
content:
Price action seen at the London Close typically sees a retracement:
- Off the high of the day on bullish days.
- Off the low of the day on bearish days.
- There is typically a five-minute optimal trade entry (OTE) setup in these conditions.
- It is very important to remember that these moves are very short-term in nature, not comparable to a
London Open or New York Open.
- Trades typically yield 10, 15, maybe even 20 pips. Anything more than 20 pips is a rarity.

- LOW OF DAY AND OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY
On an Aussie Dollar chart, within the ICT London Close Killzone:
- The market creates the low of the day as expected.
- It then provides an optimal trade entry long and trades higher, offering an easy 20 pips.
- While they won't always look like this, it's important to see how the daily range is encapsulated.
- The highs or lows generally form between 10 o'clock and noon New York time.

section_title: RULE OF THUMB FOR BULLISH DAYS
content:
For note-taking and completeness:
- If we are bullish and the daily close is going to be high relative to its opening (an up-close day), the
London Close is typically when the high of the day is formed between 10 o'clock and noon.
- There will be times when this isn't the case, but this general rule of thumb will serve you well.

section_title: LONDON CLOSE CONTINUATION POINTS AND REVERSALS
content:
The London Close can create continuation points for swings that trade well into New York afternoon hours.
- If we're bullish overall on the day, instead of creating the high of the day in bullish markets or bullish days,
many times the London Close connects to create a continuation pattern.

- CONTINUATION PATTERN (5-MINUTE BASIS)
On a Dollar Yen chart:
- The market traded higher initially during the day, followed by a consolidation.
- Inside the consolidation, an optimal trade entry (OTE) is observed on a 5-minute basis.
- When studying the London Close, it is essential to look at it from a 5-minute perspective; anything higher
will not provide the necessary detail.
- This OTE long creates the continuation of the overall trend and a nice extrapolated move.

section_title: NEW YORK AND LONDON CLOSE OVERLAP CHARACTERISTICS
content:
The direction of the day or week can change during the London Close.
- For the New York session, typically you will see a continuation of what's seen in London overnight.
- If London is bullish, we expect New York to continue moving bullishly, and the London Close would be the
high of the day.
- If London is bearish, we've seen London bearish, then New York bearish, we're really looking for the
London Close to be the low of the day between 10 o'clock and noon.

- LIQUIDITY POOL REVERSAL
In situations where the market creates an institutional side liquidity pool, such as a double top:
- The London Close, on this particular day, creates the high of the day.
- It also forms a reversal point for the rest of that day and for the next day, leading to price moving
aggressively lower.

Understanding the engineering of how price moves from the short-term is beneficial, even if not intraday
trading, to then work from that price point higher into higher time frames.
- It's important to know these things for entries.
- It's important to know these things for managing positions.
- The characteristics shown provide the 'DNA' of how a daily bar or candle is formed on bullish and bearish
days.

The times you're going to be unfruitful using this information are:
- When we have a 'Z day,' where price goes up and down in quiet consolidation.
- A 'seek and destroy day,' which is a really wild, choppy up-and-down day where price doesn't really go
anywhere until the last portion of the day, then runs out the stops unexpectedly.
- Accept that some scenarios, even if they warrant an entry on a demo account, may not come to fruition.

section_title: DEFINING THE DAILY RANGE AND POWER OF THREE
content:
By studying the Asian session, the London session, the New York session, and now the London Close
Killzone, we have completed the definition of the daily range.
- This defines the four reference points that make up the 'Power of Three': the Open, the High, the Low, and
the Close.
- It also explains the three phenomena that take place inside of those four price points: Accumulation,
Manipulation, and Distribution.
- This knowledge provides everything at our disposal for defining the daily range, though it does not define
daily bias or long-term direction.
